titre: How to MACRO In LOW ELO in Season 15!
transcription: In this guide, we are going to be teaching you the best way to dominate the map in low elo with macro tactics that are guaranteed to work every single game. Now, if you've ever watched a challenger player smurfing in your ELO and making it look extremely easy, then you've probably had the thought at some point or another that their opponents, they don't look nearly as competent as what you go up against. The same players in your ELO, they look terrible compared to them. And that is for one simple reason. Great players don't just mechanically outplay their opponents, but they control the pace of the game. So, everyone looks like a headless chicken when they're playing against them. And that is the sole reason we want to teach you everything that is in this guide. The macro strategies that challengers actually use to make every low elo player look completely clueless. Now, to start, the most important thing to understand about macro is that it almost always revolves around one singular concept. pushing waves into your opponent's tower, or at least as close as you can get to it. Now, this is the foundation of almost every single macro decision, whether it's warding, whether it's objectives, team fighting, and so on. It all begins with a pushed wave. But why, though? Well, if you think about it, the point of macro is to get consistent results in solo Q. By pushing a wave into your opponent's tower, you're forcing them to respond in a particular way, which makes games a whole lot more predictable and a whole lot more controllable. The first decision your opponents are going to have to make is whether they're going to go kill that wave or not. Minions are worth gold and experience. And if they don't collect that wave, they're going to miss that entire value. So, they either go farm the wave and force themselves into one single area of the map, or they ignore it and completely miss out on crucial resources. Not only is missing resources going to be a threat, but a minion wave also represents the opportunity for you to attack that tower. So, if they go do something else, then you can also threaten to push in and potentially take towers because they disregarded the minion wave. Almost every single macro decision you can possibly make should always begin with you thinking about a minion wave. Now, there is a reason that even during an incredibly tense team fight that a game analyst, they're typically going to immediately look at and discuss minions first. >> They're going to lose a bot wave here. Like, look at this. You're fighting Herold and look at that bleeding bot wave. Look at that bleeding midwave. Nothing is ever good. You're always losing gold on every play. Now, obviously, that's professional play where team-based macro is going to be wildly different from your own. Because let's be real, your solo Q teammates can't even group with Baron, let alone play around waves before it. So, when you hear the same old advice, like, just push waves before an objective, bro, you already know that's not going to work for you. And we get that, which is exactly why we work together with Coach Rogue to put together three brand new courses, including one fully dedicated to macro. taking these same high elo strategies and showing you how to actually apply them in your ELO. Because at Skillcap, we don't just teach you what's correct to do in theory. We focus on teaching you what actually works in your games at your rank with your teammates. And we're so confident in that that we backed it up for over a decade. As if you don't climb at least five divisions while actively using our service, you get every penny back, no questions asked. So, click the link in the description below to gain access to a special one-time discount and get the rank that you have always wanted this season. So, now that you understand the importance of waves to macro, let's talk about the first step to controlling the map as much as you possibly can in solo queue. Now, ideally, what you and your team should strive to do is push in all three lanes constantly. There's a couple of benefits to this. The first is the obvious and most important one. You don't want to share farm with your other teammates. top, mid, and ADC should all be forming a lane of their own most of the time to get as strong as possible. The second benefit is that the more waves your team pushes in, the more likely it is that your opponents make a mistake. Maybe the enemy players in top and mid are covering the pushed waves properly. But if just one single person messes up and maybe leaves a lane alone when they shouldn't have, they could suffer some massive consequences, which can give your team a huge advantage. So, the goal is to push three lanes to spread farm amongst your team and to make it way more likely your opponent to make a macro mistake because you're putting pressure in so many different areas of the map. Now, this is where most guides, including ours, will tell you about ideal lane assignments. Now, the way that the advice goes is typically that the ADC and support should all stay mid. Then, the player with teleport should play on the opposite side of the upcoming objective. For example, if top has teleport and Baron is the current most important objective, they should be the one pushing bottom, ready to TP to the fight. And that would leave the mid laner to catch top where they can quickly rotate to join the fight at Baron. Now, while it is good to understand what should be happening in solo Q, you need to be flexible, especially in the lower brackets where it's unlikely that you're going to be in proper lane states all the time. For example, here we have a Kog'aw who's one lane. Normally, as an ADC, you'd transition to mid lane now and you'd swap your mid laner down to bot. But the Renekton laning in mid doesn't want to swap even though it would benefit him to do so. Sometimes your teammates, they're going to be stubborn. They're human after all. They're tilted maybe at how the game's going or whatever it is. Solo Q is always going to be super super random due to the variety of teammates that you're really going to get. You can complain about it or you can just do what Kog'aw did and simply just go to bottom instead. This is the number one guideline that you want to keep in mind at all times in regards to your macro. Don't overthink proper lane assignments. As long as you can push out a wave to around here, a little past the halfway point in lane, and put even just a little bit of pressure, you can be in charge of the side lane, even as an ADC. Lane assignments aren't nearly as important as simply having three lanes being properly farmed and pushed out most of the time. Okay, enough of that though. Push, push, push, push. But what happens now? What do you do after you've pushed a wave into the opponent's side of the map? That brings us to the most basic, most common, but also one of the most effective macro strategies in the entire game. the push and rotate. After pushing, you just head on over to where the rest of your teammates are and you try to make a play with a numbers advantage. Remember, this is because someone on the enemy team is currently stuck farming the wave that you pushed into their towers. So, you've got a oneperson advantage over the enemy just for a little bit, which you should honestly take full advantage of. Now, while the push and rotate is a very simple macro play, it is extremely effective, especially if you understand a couple of key concepts to take full advantage of it. First, you have to remember as you make your play that you're on a timer here. Technically, what should happen is that while you're grouping with your team, the enemy who is stuck catching the wave now pushes it to your tower, and then they get their own chance to rotate to their team. Technically, this is kind of called taking turns. You rotate, make a play with your team, then they push the wave into your tower. So, now it's their turn to make a play, then you get to make a play again, and so on and so forth. Now, that's not necessarily how it'll go in solo Q most of the time. We all get that. But understanding the idea is important here. All you need to remember is that you're on a timer to get back to catch the wave that they're pushing into your tower. Just make sure you don't group for too long though. As soon as you see the wave getting close to your tower, you really need to head back before you miss too many minions. An important tip to make this favor you though is to remember that the more you push a wave out, then the more time you're going to have to group up with your team. For example, Set just pushed a wave pretty deep into the enemy side of the map. Then he bases and teleports to bottom to dive the enemy duo to score a bunch of kills for his team. All the while Malight was trying to push the way back into Set's tower as we just discussed. But since set initially pushed it so far into Malight's side of the map, he had all the time in the world to make the play base and then run back to top before the wave had even gotten to his tower. This process is precisely how good players win games so dang consistently. Instead of making random plays, they follow these simple concepts to maximize their farm and map pressure all at the same time. Now, one final thing to talk about here is the elephant in the room. What do you do if your opponents just don't catch the wave? What if they just follow you as you rotate across the map to your team? This is pretty straightforward here. You don't have to do anything. Remember, missing a whole wave is a ton of gold and experience lost. So, if you do nothing, you're getting ahead for free. However, just keep in mind that the relative value of waves does go down as the game goes on. A minion wave at level 9 is worth way more than a wave at level 17, for example. So, your opponent's missing waves at around 15 minutes or so is going to be a huge win. But a single wave really isn't worth that much later into the game. Now, that being said, as the value of waves does go down, their threat to enemy towers goes up. As you get more and more items, most champions can begin deleting enemy towers in just a few seconds. And this is why pushing waves is relevant at all stages of the game. Early on, it's more about threatening to deny resources. Later into the match, it becomes more about threatening the enemy base instead. You can do a ton of damage with just one minion wave, which all brings us to our next macro strategy, split pushing. Everyone knows that champions like Jax, Trindir, or Fiora are the side lane monsters of this game. But any champion with good wave clear and tower damage can split push, even ADCs in the right circumstances, of course. So, let's talk about some guidelines to make your split pushing way more consistent so you know how and when to do it. First things first, let's say you've pushed a side lane wave and you've noticed no one is going to be here to defend the tower. So, you have a choice. Do you rotate to your team like we discussed before or do you stay and you hit the tower? Here's a simple way to make that decision. Remember that towers provide absolutely no value to you unless you completely destroy them. Turret plates, they aren't a thing after 14 minutes. So, doing half a tower's HP, it's going to be pointless for the most part. Now, on the other hand, towers do provide a ton of gold if you do manage to destroy it. So, just ask yourself that question. If you see no one defending it, can I take this before someone gets here to defend? If yes, stay and get the tower. If not, rotate to your team. In this particular situation here, that decision might seem a little bit weird because the enemy team is pushing so aggressively. But keep in mind that your teammates, they are way more likely to play well if they see you taking a tower for free. So, for example, Anivia Zyra knows that if she just clears this wave right here, they're going to come out ahead because Anivvia is taking the bot lane tower uncontested. Split pushing always carries a risk, though, because you're not with your teammates. So, they might all die and then blame you for not grouping. But that's a pretty dangerous mindset to have. You definitely shouldn't play in fear that if your teammates mess up, it's going to be all your fault because you left them alone. They're in the same ELO bracket that you are. They want to win, and they are just as likely to play well as they are to die. Don't base your decisions on what they might or might not do. However, you do want to think about what your teammates can do with the pressure you put on by splitting. This is a concept that we delve really deep into over at our site. Let's start off our section on the midame with probably the best concept I ever came up with, and that's the idea of tempo lines. Tempo lines is essentially a tool you can use in the midame in order to see where to go and what to do in an attempt to better sync up with your teammates. and it is by far the most realistic way to play when you're playing in solo cube, especially in lower elos where your teammates are crazy. Again, if you want to truly master the concepts we're teaching in this guide, you should definitely check out our full courses using the link below. And that's because understanding these types of concepts can allow you to make macro calls that no one else is going to be making in your ELO. For example, here we have a Fed Oriana pushing top. Now, if you ask most low ELO players, they're going to tell you that she probably shouldn't sit there and hit the tower. That's because she's an immobile mage with no vision deep on the enemy side of the map. But Oriana knows better here and is actively thinking about what her own team is capable of rather than just focusing on her own safety. Right now, with Dragon and Oticon being contestable objectives, she's putting the enemy team into a super uncomfortable situation by pushing this tower. They either go contest the objectives, letting her get the tower for free, or they try to collapse on her instead. By considering her own teammates positions on the map, Oriana has instantly won the game in a single move. The enemy team felt pressured to send multiple people to defend versus her, letting the rest of the team secure two major objectives completely for free without even needing to a random team fight. Now, as you can clearly see, all good macro begins with a pushed wave, which gives you options for how to play the map. While at the same time, it makes your opponent's options predictable, which might be some of the concern here if you're playing a role that doesn't have the option to constantly push, like supports and junglers. These two roles obviously can't just sit in a lane and push like the other three can, but that doesn't mean everything you've learned thus far is pointless. Your goal is to just instead help your teammates with their pushing if needed, or to get more value out of their wave crashes in general. Here's a really good example of what we mean. Here we have Janna pathing towards mid and pushing up really aggressively looking to zone the enemy team while also providing vision in the area. Then she hovers Jin for a bit to make sure he can push and crash this wave into the tower. As we just discussed this entire time, your goal as a jungler andor support is to create a bridge from one pushing lane to another. What you want to do is hover that area placing vision or just contesting the enemy team from entering. This way you connect two pushing lanes to each other. In doing so, think about how that helps your pushing teammates. For starters, it allows them to push and roam way more effectively with all the vision in the area. One player could go mid or the other could go to bottom really easily without fear of dying randomly in the jungle as they rotate. So, back to our real example here. As you can see, Morgana and Janna are currently creating that same bridge between mid and top. This bridge also lets you quickly react to the pressure your teammates are creating. Because there's only a level eight Zed defending versus Atrox, Morgana and Janna take the initiative to cut him off from retreating back to his base. And then they eventually kill the Zed and take the tower to boot. All thanks to our bridge concept. Now, of course, there is one final concern that you might have when it comes to macro. What if you and your team just can't push the wave? In 50% of the games you play, you are obviously going to be on the losing team. So, you may not be able to actually pressure the enemy team if they're the ones pushing waves into your towers. Now, we can't cover every single detail for playing from behind here, but let's try to cover some major insights that should help you try to mount a comeback. First, let me be the first to say that although losing absolutely sucks in League, there is a weird advantage that comes from your team being on the back foot. As your team loses towers and are forced further into your own base, the area of the map you're in charge of begins to shrink. On the other hand, your opponent's zone of influence grows larger and larger. That might sound bad, but it's actually kind of good for you. It means that since your team has less of the area to play in, that you'll be more tightly packed in, letting you rotate to each other a whole lot more quickly since you have less ground to cover. This often results in your team having a numbers advantage simply by virtue of always being so close to each other. The winning team is usually going to have someone far away doing something else, giving you a chance to potentially try to come back by biting with more numbers. There's no guarantees when playing from behind, but it is an advantage that you have to consider. Another potential mistake that your opponents might make when ahead is that players tend to overgroup to secure objectives. Now, what we mean by this is that you typically don't need all five players if you're winning to secure something on the map. You only need three or four while the others defend other areas. A huge mistake many lower elo players make is trying to contest something when they can't fight their stronger opponents anyway. Notice how all five of the blue teams players are in this area taking Oticon. One of the best tactics for coming back in games like these is known as cross mapping. Basically, this just means that while your opponents take something on one side of the map that you take something somewhere else on the map. Now, players usually think that you need to cross mapap something big for it to be worth it. Like in this game, there's no other objective or tower for the red team to take while the blue side takes Oticon. there's waves and jungle camps spawning that they could contest instead. You don't need a big cross map to be effective. At the very least, you should trade something on the map, even if it is a small trade, rather than just walking in to die, falling even further behind. And all of that is going to wrap up what we have time to cover in this single video. But as we've mentioned, if you want to learn all the intricacies of macro, then all you need to do is sign up to Skill Cap with the link below. Anyway, thanks for watching and we'll see you soon.

titre: EVERYTHING You NEED to know about MACRO in SEASON 16!

So, possibly the biggest change that players have yet to become adjusted to from season 15 to season 16 is the priority you should place on void grubs based on all the changes that Riot added. In fact, grubs seem to be even stronger than ever since there's turret plates on every tower now, indicating that the objective that helps you destroy structures would become even stronger than before. Except, if you compare the win rate between taking dragon versus taking grubs, you're going to notice a huge discrepancy there with dragon being the much better early objective. So, how in the world did grubs go from an absurdly strong objective in season 15 to supposedly being a whole lot worse now despite all signs pointing to them being broken with these changes? Well, it has to do with the fact that the early game is significantly less important these days compared to before, and this is just the tip of the iceberg. In season 15, securing early grubs gave your team a significant tower pushing advantage which would likely secure you the first tower of the game which in turn would often guarantee your team feats of strength. These bonus stats then help you control the map, take an early autocon, and snowball the game from there. The grubs downfall, it doesn't stop there though. First tower is far less important now, but so are turret plates. Before, you had until 14 minutes in the game to secure all 15 plates on the outer towers before they disappeared and the gold was gone forever. Grubs used to be fantastic to try and rush them down before 14 minutes, but as you all know, plates last forever in season 16. You have all the time in the world to eventually secure plates which in turn makes grubs even less valuable. Now, we're going to talk about towers in a second, but first, let's discuss what your real priority should be in every game. You know how you're often going to trade grubs for dragon in your games or vice versa. Well, in this season, you should always prioritize dragons in your games. Now, as you've all experienced, most games do tend to end when one team gets dragon soul. If you're ahead, you're usually waiting for soul to get enough power to finish the match. And if you're behind, well, League's comeback mechanics are pretty good, meaning you always have a chance as long as your opponents haven't taken soul yet. [music] So, as the winning team, getting dragon early is far better than grubs because you can threaten soul 5 minutes earlier. And if you're losing, then taking an early dragon delays the enemy team from securing soul for another 5 minutes which is more time to maybe pick up some shutdowns and maybe make a comeback. Now, as for grubs, they should be more of a luxury objective this time around. It's not that you don't want to take them, but more so that you shouldn't go out of your way to secure them in this meta. For example, supports used to always recall before 8 minutes to go towards the grub fight. That shouldn't be something you do every single game these days. It's especially true because of the way that towers have fundamentally changed in season 16. As you all know, the overgrowth mechanic builds up on towers for a huge burst of instant damage, [music] but more importantly, since all towers have plates now. Then for the rest of the game you have to play around the turret plating mechanic. Now, if you haven't learned how this works yet, basically, it just makes it so that each time you destroy a turret plate on a tower, they get a temporary buff. This gives them extra armor and extra magic resistance just for a little bit, and this also scales based on the amount of players that are around when a plate falls. So, towers are extra durable if you try to kill them with more players around you. This combination of upfront damage from overgrowth build-up and towers getting tankier as plates go down encourages players to not just sit there hitting a tower for too long. After one or two plates, the tower is much, much tankier, making it much more meaningless to sit there hitting it unless you're in the late stages of the game. Now, this is the reason for why grubs are technically worse this season. They're meant to help you destroy towers by giving you extra DPS as you hit them. But, this is counter-intuitive to how you want to play these days. And as a general rule, you should be aiming to destroy turrets in two or three attempts rather than all at once. You use the overgrowth to take one or two plates, then come back later and take one or two more, and then you finally destroy the tower later on. But, it turns out the biggest threat to towers isn't necessarily overgrowth. It's the new overpowered homeguards a lot of the player base has been complaining about. Players are getting back out onto the map so dang fast that it's hard to leave your turrets alone for more than a couple of seconds before someone's already back there ready to destroy them. But, what most players haven't realized yet is what Homeguard's actually changed when it comes to macro decisions in lane. In previous seasons, if you forced your opponent to base, you could gain some huge advantages pushing the lane, taking turret plates, and denying the minions. But now, more often than not, this can actually be a big mistake as Homeguards allow players to get back to lane so much faster this season, which is why good players are already learning and adapting to the new Season 16 timers. And here's what you need to know. It takes around 12 seconds to get back to mid lane from the fountain. And it takes about 17 seconds to get back to either top or bot lane tier one. Combine this with the eight seconds of recall, and the time it take for somebody to base and get back to lane is night and day compared to last season. And this is where many players really do slip up now. As although you might be playing well in lane and forcing your opponent to base, in a lot of cases, it can actually end up worse for you now, especially with the new turret changes that we just mentioned. As although that first plate is so easy to take or that overgrowth feels so incredibly satisfying to pop, before you know it, your opponent is already back in lane with full health and an item advantage while you're now stuck in lane. So, just to put this all into perspective after all of these things, let's say you watch your opponent complete their base in mid. This means that they're going to be back in lane in roughly 12 seconds. And since it takes about eight seconds for your own recall to go off, you technically only have four seconds of free time before you need to base. Since if you haven't completed it by then, it could be canceled by your opponent. So, the next time you force somebody out of lane, don't get greedy now. You do not have as big of an advantage as you once did in Season 15. Often just pushing as fast as possible and then matching their base is, sadly, the best play you can make nowadays. So, one of the main changes to Season 16 was that, as we know, games are now starting a whole lot sooner with minion spawning at 30 seconds and camps now at 55 seconds. And this single change is causing so many players to get caught out by early ganks. And yet, there's a super simple way to ensure that you never die to a gank in the early game. And it all starts with the most overpowered ward [music] in the whole game. This, right here at Raptors. And this early Raptor ward should ideally be dropped by your mid laner, but even your top laner or jungler can do it as well. Now, why this is so important is that this ward tells you everything that you need in the early game. What camp the enemy jungler started in, where they're pathing, and which particular lane needs to be worried about early ganks. And here's specifically how this works. The second you see the enemy jungler on this ward, press tab. Every jungle camp in the game is worth four CS. So, let's say that I'm on blue team. If the enemy jungler shows on this ward with 12 CS, they've cleared three camps and are pathing up. If they have eight CS, or they're just starting Raptors, then they're obviously pathing down. Red side works the exact same way, it's just mirrored. Which this then brings us to the most important early game timer that you have to be aware about. 2 minutes and 55 seconds, as this is now the time that Scuttle Crab spawns. With many junglers finishing their clear at around this time, or slightly before. A neat trick to track this even easier as a laner is to use the cannon minion as a landmark. Once this dies, you should look to ward, as that will be around 2 minutes and 30 seconds, which will cover you from any potential early ganks. The next timer you need to know is 3 minutes and 45 seconds, which replaced last season's 4 minutes and 20 second timer. As once you know where a jungler starts, their early pathing becomes incredibly predictable. As 3 minutes and 45 seconds is when their first camp respawns, which instantly tells you where they're likely to be on the map next. Typically giving you the green light to look for any aggressive plays. And if you find timers like this interesting, that kind of lay out the game for you, we have an entire video breaking down the most important timers that you need to know for season 16 inside our brand new macro course. And speaking of timers, there's an even more critical one most of the player base really isn't taking advantage of yet, and it has to do with something you've probably noticed this season. Way fewer solo laners are running teleport. For quite a few seasons now, we had teleport be almost mandatory for top and mid laners to take, usually resulting in four teleports in each game. But due to homeguards being so effective at getting players back to lane, combat summoners are far more frequent than before. This is something you can actually exploit in solo queue by tracking TP. One of the most common fundamentals of macro is to always put the player with TP on the opposite side of the objective. So, if Baron was the next objective to contest, you'd have your top laner with TP push bot. The goal is to be able to have pressure in all three lanes while still being able to contest the objective with all five players if needed. Now, in previous seasons, this wasn't something you really had to think about all that much because both top and mid were likely running teleport as a summoner. There was almost always someone on your team with it available. So, you always had someone who could side lane away from the objective. In season 16, teams are going to often find themselves without a teleport in draft. Instead, top laners rely on the free one they get once their quest is complete. But this is what you can take advantage of. If you watch any competitive play, you're going to often still see both top laners almost always running TP, and the mid laners still run it in most games as well. The reason for this is pretty simple. Remember, we spent a while discussing how dragons are without a doubt the best win condition right now since Autocon and feats were removed. And dragons spawns every 5 minutes. Pro teams know that if you don't have TP up for dragon, then you would just lose so many side lane waves and turret plates every time the objective spawns. You can see how there's usually two players in the furthest lane, and then they both TP to dragon to make sure no farm is lost or their tower gets threatened. Now, obviously, we're not playing competitive professional games, but But doesn't mean this concept isn't important for solo queue. Keep in mind that especially in lower ELO brackets, players are running combat summoner spells far more often. So, unlike before when there's always a TP or two up, you usually only have to track a single teleport from the enemy top laner, which is on a massive 7-minute cooldown. Here's how you can take advantage of this. Their TPs do not match with every dragon spawn. So, if you ever see the enemy top laner TP to a fight, then you should time it in the chat. And make sure you do the control A plus control C chat tech to copy it quickly so you can paste that timer later. Then, once dragon is close to respawning, your team will know they have a massive advantage since the enemy TP will have a 2-minute cooldown remaining. If you push top lane aggressively as dragon is spawning, the enemy top laner is not going to have any choice but to stay in that lane despite not having a way to get to dragon quickly. Then, if your team has TP, the player pushing can quickly join the fight guaranteeing a 5 V 4 advantage. Or if the enemy top just decides to walk to the fight normally, your teammate can push multiple waves aggressively in top, take two or three turret plates, and then TP to the dragon anyway getting a huge gold lead. You could also decide as a team to give up a single dragon to allow that teammate to take even more plates and waves [music] in that uncontested lane. If you have an ally who's really good at pushing or scales really [music] well like maybe Kale. Basically, timing TP is actually useful again in games where your opponent's only have the free teleport from the top quest available. The cooldown is so long, it'll rarely be up for the most important objective in a game which can give your team a huge advantage. Misunderstanding how to value the quest rewards is without a doubt one of the biggest hurdles most players are really going to have to get through in this new meta. Just because the top quest gives you a free TP, it doesn't mean there aren't consequences to not taking it as a base summoner spell with a lower cooldown. And playing around all the quest in general is definitely where a whole lot of players are struggling the most this season. As we all know, you gain points toward your role quest by farming minions, killing champions, taking turret plates, and passively over time as the game progresses. All of which then gets doubled when in your assigned lane. So, naturally, most players come to the obvious conclusion that you should stay in your lane until your quest is completed, right? Well, don't get me wrong, finishing your role quest ASAP is definitely important, but the majority of players are focusing on these quests so insanely hard that they actively end up grieving themselves and their team because of it. But, here's where things get absolutely crazy. Watch Darius. Instead of pathing to mid, collecting the wave, and defending the turret, what does he do? He walks straight up to top. Why? Well, as he explains, he can't go mid because he hasn't completed his role quest. Yeah, you're right. If he goes mid lane with quest for one single wave, I guess the game's just over. That's totally right, bro. What should happen, and what would usually happen prior to season 16, was that Darius would path mid, collect the wave, and then walk back up to top while Zoe bases and then heads back to mid for the next wave. This way Darius gets to soak the wave, Garen doesn't get any plates, and no resources on the map would go to waste. And we're seeing this more and more this season. Players are either hesitant to rotate or even refusing to move to objectives like Grubs or Dragon all because they think leaving lane will set them too far behind. Well, here's the reality.
